schools.summary: Jude calls himself 'servant of Jesus Christ' and 'brother of James' (the Jerusalem leader), not 'apostle' nor 'Judas of James' (Luke 6:16); the readers sanctified by the Father, preserved in Christ, and called — the threefold keeping grounding the letter.
Calvin: 'servant of Christ' here with respect to his apostleship; Wesley: the highest glory any can aspire to.

'Be multiplied' (plethyntheie, optative; cf 1 Pet 1:2; 2 Pet 1:2) — mercy (pardon to the unmeriting), peace (with God and conscience), love (God's and ours), asking not a bare measure but an overflowing increase.
Calvin: mercy nearly the same as grace — grace the effect of mercy, God embracing us because he pities our misery.

He meant to write of the 'common salvation' (Titus 1:4) but 'had necessity' to urge contending for 'the faith once delivered' — given once for all, a fixed deposit, entrusted to the whole church to guard; the contending vigorous yet (Wesley) humble and loving.
Henry: the design to establish them in the faith, especially in times of notorious opposition.

They 'crept in' (pareisedysan, slipped in by a side door) under fair pretences; 'before of old ordained/written of' for condemnation; turning grace into 'lasciviousness' (licence) and 'denying the Lord' by doctrine or life (cf 2 Pet 2:1).
Calvin: a peculiar assault of Satan, demanding the church take up arms.

Reminding 'though ye know all things once for all' (hapax); the Lord saved Israel from Egypt yet 'destroyed them that believed not' — deliverance no security for the unbelieving; textual variant 'the Lord'/'Jesus.'
Henry: outward privilege cannot secure those who fall into unbelief; none may presume on past mercies.

The angels 'kept not their first estate' (arche, principality/rank), forsook their habitation, and are 'reserved in everlasting chains under darkness' for judgment (cf 2 Pet 2:4) — the highest creatures not escaping rebellion's doom.
Calvin: an argument from greater to less — if God spared not angels, he will not forgive men who depart from his grace.

themes: angels who kept not their estate

schools.summary: Sodom, Gomorrah, and the cities of the plain (Admah, Zeboiim; Zoar spared, Deut 29:23) 'set forth for an example,' their fiery overthrow a 'type of eternal fire' (cf 2 Pet 2:6) — the third historic judgment on ungodliness.
Calvin: God punishes all the ungodly without distinction; the fire a type of the eternal.

The 'filthy dreamers' (enypniazomenoi) of v4, in spite of the warnings, commit a threefold sin answering the three examples — 'defile the flesh' (like Sodom), 'despise dominion' (like the angels), 'speak evil of dignities'; sin as filthiness and delusion (Henry).

Michael (Dan 10:13; Rev 12:7), even disputing with the devil over Moses' body, 'durst not bring a railing accusation' but said 'The Lord rebuke thee' (Zech 3:2); the dispute likely from tradition/the Assumption of Moses, cited as true; the highest angel's restraint shaming the railers.
Gill identifies Michael as Christ himself ('who is as God'); most take him as the created archangel.

debates.question: Who is Michael, and whence the dispute over Moses' body?

sides.position: the created archangel of Daniel and Revelation; the story from tradition or the Assumption of Moses, cited as true
Michael is Christ himself, 'who is as God'

schools.summary: A double folly — they 'speak evil of what they know not' (heavenly things beyond them), and what they know 'naturally, as brute beasts' (aloga zoa, by instinct) they use to corrupt themselves (cf 2 Pet 2:12); creatures of instinct, not spiritual men.

themes: speak evil of what they know not

schools.summary: The one 'woe' in Jude, with three parallels — 'the way of Cain' (envy, hatred, false worship, Gen 4), 'the error of Balaam for reward' (covetous corruption, Num 22), 'the gainsaying of Korah' (rebellion against God's leaders, Num 16); exposing their envy, greed, and insubordination and forecasting their doom.

'Hidden rocks' (spilades, submerged reefs) at the 'love-feasts' (agapais), feeding without fear; 'clouds without water' (promise, no rain), 'trees twice dead, plucked up by the roots' (dead by nature and by apostasy, beyond reviving) — deceptive show and inner emptiness.

'Wild waves' (kymata agria) 'foaming out' (epaphrizonta) their shame (Isa 57:20), casting up mire; 'wandering stars' (erratic, not the fixed lights that guide) for whom is 'reserved the blackness of darkness for ever' — images from sea and sky sealing their doom.

Enoch, 'the seventh from Adam' (Gen 5; Heb 11:5), 'prophesied of these' — the Lord coming with myriads of his saints; the doom of the ungodly announced from the earliest age, the inspired citation guaranteeing the saying's truth.
Calvin: more likely an unwritten prophecy preserved by tradition than taken from an apocryphal book; Clarke notes the Book of Enoch.

debates.question: Did Jude cite the apocryphal Book of Enoch?

sides.position: an unwritten prophecy preserved by tradition, confirmed true by the Spirit through Jude
drawn from the Book of Enoch, the inspired use guaranteeing its truth

themes: the Lord cometh with his saints

schools.summary: The drumbeat of 'ungodly' — the Lord comes 'to execute judgment' on all and 'convict' the ungodly of both their ungodly deeds and their 'hard speeches' against him (cf Rom 1:18); an exhaustive arraignment of ungodliness in act and word.

'Murmurers' (gongystai, like wilderness Israel) and 'complainers' (mempsimoiroi, fault-finders with their lot), ruled by lust, with 'great swelling words' (hyperonka), yet flattering the influential 'because of advantage' — discontent, pride, and mercenary self-interest.
Henry: a murmuring, complaining temper betokens a heart at odds with God.

'But, beloved' turns from the intruders to the faithful; 'remember' (mnesthete) the apostles' foretelling (close to 2 Pet 3:2) — the most recent prophecy added to Enoch's most ancient, so warnings from first and last ages confirm such deceivers were expected; Jude distinguishes himself among the apostles (Wesley).

The apostles foretold 'mockers in the last time' walking after 'ungodly lusts' (close to 2 Pet 3:3; cf 1 Tim 4:1; 2 Tim 3:1); the 'last time' the gospel age from Christ's first coming; the intruders precisely the predicted men, their rise vindicating the apostolic word.

Three traits mounting to the root — 'they who make separations' (apodiorizontes, dividers, often under pretence of superior wisdom), 'sensual' (psychikoi, soulish/animal), and decisively 'having not the Spirit'; the fatal lack from which their schisms and fleshly lives flow.

'But ye, beloved' — over against the Spirit-less intruders, 'building up yourselves' (epoikodomountes, the temple metaphor, 1 Cor 3:9; Eph 2:20) on the 'most holy faith' (the faith once delivered) and 'praying in the Holy Ghost' (by the Spirit they lacked) — faith the foundation, prayer the breath.

themes: building up on your most holy faith

schools.summary: 'Keep yourselves' (teresate, urgent) 'in the love of God' (chiefly our love to him) — balancing v1's 'kept' (divine preservation and human responsibility, cf Phil 2:12); 'looking for the mercy of Christ unto eternal life' — perseverance resting at last on mercy, not merit.

themes: keep yourselves in the love of God
looking for the mercy of Christ

schools.summary: On some 'have compassion, making a difference' — the wavering and weak, led astray but tractable, met with tender mercy to win them back; treating the erring according to each one's case (Calvin); textual variation 'have mercy'/'convict' (eleate/elegchete).

Others 'save with fear, pulling them out of the fire' (ek pyros harpazontes, a brand from the burning, Zech 3:2; Amos 4:11) by alarming appeals; yet 'hating even the garment spotted by the flesh' — shunning sin's contagion like a plague-garment; courageous yet self-guarding love.

God 'able to keep you from falling' (aptaistous, sure-footed, not stumbling into the intruders' ruin) and to 'present you faultless' (spotless) before his glory 'with exceeding joy' (Rom 16:25); the answer to the letter's anxiety — those who keep themselves are kept by God.
Calvin: human labours avail nothing but through the power of God; Henry: God as willing as able to keep us, through mercy.

themes: able to keep you from falling

schools.summary: 'To the only wise God our Saviour' (mono theo sotheri, Rom 16:27; 1 Tim 1:17) be 'glory, majesty, dominion, power' now and ever — against the false teachers' corrupt wisdom, God alone truly wise and the Author of salvation; the better text adds 'through Jesus Christ our Lord'; sealed with 'Amen.'
Gill applies 'Saviour' to Christ, the wisdom of God; Calvin: all our labours avail only through the power of God, to whom the glory.
